title: Extracting the billing module out of the monolith into its own service
goal: Produce an ordered, risk-controlled plan to extract billing from the monolith into a standalone service.

branches.reason: root planning line for the billing extraction
decompose the extraction into parallel workstreams
API seam / anti-corruption layer workstream, explored in parallel
verify cross-workstream sequencing and data-consistency risk
revisit the stashed dual-write branch after discovering a dependency, then converge on the ordered plan
side investigation into outbox exactly-once / idempotent delivery, forked off the API-seam verify

units.full: We need to extract the billing module out of the monolith and stand it up as its own service. Billing is the most coupled domain in the codebase: invoicing, payment capture, subscription state, and the ledger all read and write the same shared Postgres tables that orders, accounts, and reporting also touch. The whole point of the extraction is to give billing an independent deploy cadence and a clear ownership boundary, but the coupling means I cannot just lift the code. I have to untangle the data layer, define a hard service boundary, and move live traffic without dropping a single payment. Let me frame this as a migration with explicit workstreams and a reversible cutover, not a big-bang rewrite.
units.thought: Extract the billing module from the monolith into its own service; the hard part is the shared Postgres coupling, so plan it as a reversible migration with explicit workstreams, not a big-bang rewrite.

units.full: Before I plan anything, I should be honest about the current state. Billing today is roughly forty thousand lines spread across three packages, and it shares the same database connection pool as the rest of the monolith. There are direct foreign keys from the orders table into the invoices table, and reporting runs analytical joins straight across billing tables every night. Three other teams have code paths that read billing rows synchronously. So this is not a clean module with a thin interface; it is deeply woven in. That tells me the dominant risk is data, not code, and the migration order has to be driven by how I split the database, not by how I move the source files.
units.thought: Billing shares the monolith's DB pool, has foreign keys from orders into invoices, and three teams read its rows synchronously; the dominant risk is data, so the database split must drive the migration order.

units.full: Let me decompose the extraction into the workstreams that can run with some independence. I see four. First, the database split: give billing its own schema or database and establish dual-write so both stores stay consistent during the transition. Second, the API seam: build an anti-corruption layer so the monolith talks to billing only through a versioned interface, never the tables. Third, the traffic cutover: feature-flag-gated, percentage-based rollout that shifts read and write traffic onto the new service. Fourth, the rollback plan: at every step there must be a tested way back. The database split and the API seam are the two I can genuinely explore in parallel because neither blocks the other early on.
units.thought: Decompose into four workstreams: database split with dual-write, API seam / anti-corruption layer, feature-flag-gated traffic cutover, and a rollback plan; the database split and API seam can be explored in parallel.

units.full: I will spin up two parallel investigation lines and stash the rest. The first line takes the database split and dual-write end to end, because that is where the irreducible consistency risk lives and it has the longest lead time. The second line takes the API seam and anti-corruption layer, because the monolith's call sites need a stable target before any traffic can move. Cutover and rollback I will hold off on until I understand the shape of those two, since both of them depend on decisions the first two lines produce. So: branch into the database workstream, branch into the API workstream, keep them separate, and reconcile their constraints once each has a concrete proposal.
units.thought: Spin up two parallel lines now: one for the database split and dual-write, one for the API seam / anti-corruption layer; stash cutover and rollback until those two produce concrete constraints.

units.full: Taking the database split first. The cleanest target is billing owning its own Postgres database, fully separate from the monolith's. But the foreign keys from orders into invoices block a physical separation: you cannot keep a real FK across two databases. So step one is to break those foreign keys and replace them with application-level references plus an integrity check job. That is a schema change to the monolith that has to land and bake before anything moves. Once the FK is gone, billing's tables can be migrated logically. I will copy the current billing tables into the new database with logical replication so the new store has full history before any writes are redirected.
units.thought: For the database split, billing gets its own Postgres database; first break the orders to invoices foreign keys into application-level references with an integrity job, then seed the new store via logical replication.

units.full: Now the dual-write mechanism itself. Once billing has its own database seeded by replication, I want every billing write to land in both the old monolith tables and the new billing store, atomically enough that they never diverge. The honest version is: the monolith keeps writing as it does today, and a change-data-capture stream off its write-ahead log replays into the new store, so the new store is always a faithful follower. Application-level dual-write is tempting but it creates two-phase-commit problems and partial-failure skew. CDC off the WAL is the safer primitive because it is single-source-of-truth ordered and replayable. The new billing service reads from the new store but does not yet own writes; the monolith is still authoritative.
units.thought: Implement dual-write as change-data-capture off the monolith's write-ahead log replaying into the new billing store, not application-level two-phase commit; the monolith stays authoritative while the new store follows.

units.full: I need to convince myself the dual-write stays consistent, because if the two stores drift, a customer gets double-charged or a refund vanishes, and that is the failure that ends careers. The CDC stream gives me ordering, but I need a continuous reconciliation job that compares row counts and ledger checksums between the old and new stores on a short interval and alerts on any divergence beyond zero. I also need the reconciliation to run before, during, and after cutover. The key insight is that dual-write is only safe if divergence is observable in near-real-time and the system halts the rollout automatically when reconciliation fails. Consistency here is a measured property, not an assumed one.
units.thought: Dual-write is only safe with a continuous reconciliation job comparing row counts and ledger checksums between old and new stores, alerting on any non-zero divergence and auto-halting the rollout; consistency must be measured, not assumed.

units.full: There is a backfill question hiding in the replication step. Logical replication seeds current rows, but billing has years of historical invoices and ledger entries that reporting and customer support depend on. I cannot block the cutover on copying all of history synchronously, it could take days and lock tables. So the backfill should run as a separate, throttled, resumable batch process that copies historical rows in the background while CDC keeps the recent tail current. The cutover only needs the recent, hot window to be present and consistent; the cold historical backfill can complete after traffic has moved, as long as queries that hit cold data are routed correctly during the gap.
units.thought: Backfill years of historical invoices and ledger entries as a separate throttled resumable batch job running in the background; cutover only needs the hot recent window consistent, with cold queries routed correctly until backfill completes.

units.full: Stepping back on the database line, I have a coherent shape: break the foreign keys, seed via logical replication, follow via CDC dual-write, reconcile continuously, and backfill history asynchronously. The lead time is dominated by the foreign-key removal baking in production and by the backfill duration. I will stash this line here with that summary and switch over to the API seam workstream, because I cannot finalize the cutover ordering until I know how the monolith's call sites will reach billing. Let me hold the database branch and pick up the anti-corruption layer line, then bring the two constraint sets back together.
units.thought: Database line is coherent: break FKs, seed by replication, follow via CDC dual-write, reconcile, backfill async; stash this branch and switch to the API seam workstream before finalizing cutover ordering.

units.full: Switching to the API seam, picking up from the parallel decision. Today the monolith reaches billing by importing its packages and calling functions directly, often inside the same transaction. To extract billing, every one of those call sites must instead go through a network boundary to the billing service. I do not want the monolith's internal models leaking into the billing service's contract, and I do not want billing's future schema changes to ripple back into the monolith. So I will introduce an anti-corruption layer: a thin adapter package inside the monolith that exposes the exact billing operations the monolith needs, translating between the monolith's domain types and the billing service's versioned API. Every direct import gets rerouted through this adapter.
units.thought: For the API seam, replace every direct in-process billing call in the monolith with an anti-corruption-layer adapter that translates the monolith's domain types to billing's versioned network API, isolating both sides from each other.

units.full: The subtle part of the anti-corruption layer is the in-transaction calls. Several call sites today create an order and write an invoice inside one database transaction, relying on the shared connection to make it atomic. Once billing is a separate service over the network, that atomicity is gone, and I have to redesign those flows to be eventually consistent. The pattern is to make the order commit first, then emit an event that billing consumes to create the invoice, with an outbox table in the monolith guaranteeing the event is delivered exactly once. This is a real behavior change, not just a plumbing change, and it is the part most likely to introduce a correctness bug. I will flag these transactional call sites as the highest-risk surface.
units.thought: The hardest part of the seam is the in-transaction order-plus-invoice flows: split them into order-commits-then-emits-event with an outbox for exactly-once delivery to billing; these transactional call sites are the highest-risk surface.

units.full: Let me verify the anti-corruption layer can be introduced safely and incrementally. The good news is the adapter can be landed long before any traffic moves: I reroute all direct billing imports through the adapter while the adapter still calls the same in-process billing code underneath. That is a pure refactor with no behavior change, fully testable against the existing test suite, and it shrinks the seam to a single chokepoint. Later, when the billing service is live, I only flip the adapter's implementation from in-process calls to network calls behind a feature flag. So the API seam workstream front-loads a low-risk refactor and defers the one risky flip to cutover. That sequencing is exactly what I want.
units.thought: Land the anti-corruption adapter early as a pure refactor still calling in-process billing, collapsing the seam to one chokepoint; later flip only its implementation to network calls behind a feature flag.

units.full: I now have concrete proposals from both parallel lines, so it is time to reconcile their constraints. The API seam line says: the cutover is a single adapter flip per operation, gated by a feature flag, and the riskiest operations are the transactional order-plus-invoice flows. The database line says: the new store must be seeded, following via CDC, reconciled, and the foreign keys must already be broken. The obvious coupling is that the adapter cannot flip to network writes against the new billing service until the new service actually owns its writes, which on the database side is the very last step. So the two workstreams converge at the write-cutover moment, and everything before it can proceed independently.
units.thought: Reconcile the two lines: the API adapter flip and the database write-ownership handoff converge at the write-cutover moment; everything before it proceeds independently, with the transactional flows as the riskiest operation.

units.full: Let me verify the cross-workstream sequencing as one timeline before I trust it. Phase one: break the orders-to-invoices foreign keys and let them bake; in parallel, land the anti-corruption adapter as a pure refactor. Phase two: seed the new billing database via logical replication, start CDC dual-write, and turn on continuous reconciliation; in parallel, start the async historical backfill. Phase three: bring the billing service up reading from the new store while the monolith stays authoritative for writes. Phase four: the cutover. This ordering respects every dependency I have found: nothing reads the new store before it is seeded, nothing flips the adapter before the service is up, and writes never move before reconciliation is green.
units.thought: Sequence as four phases: break FKs plus land adapter; seed plus CDC dual-write plus reconcile plus async backfill; bring billing service up reading new store; then cutover; this respects every dependency found so far.

units.full: Now the traffic cutover itself, which I had stashed. The unit of cutover is a single billing operation, not the whole service at once. For reads, I flip the adapter to read from the new billing service for a small percentage of traffic, compare its responses against the still-authoritative monolith path in shadow mode, and ramp the percentage only while the comparison stays clean. For writes, the moment of truth is transferring write ownership: the monolith stops writing billing tables, the new service becomes authoritative, and CDC reverses direction so the old tables now follow the new store for any laggard readers. Every percentage step is feature-flag-gated and instantly reversible until write ownership transfers.
units.thought: Cutover per operation, not whole-service: ramp reads to the new service in shadow-compared percentages, then transfer write ownership so the new store is authoritative and CDC reverses; every step feature-flag-gated and reversible until writes move.

units.full: The feature-flag gating deserves a closer look because it is what makes the cutover reversible. Each billing operation needs an independent flag with at least three states: route to monolith, route to new service in shadow, route to new service authoritative. The flags must be evaluated per request, changeable without a deploy, and observable so I can see exactly what percentage is on which path at any instant. Critically, the flags have to fail safe: if the flag service is unreachable, the default must be the old monolith path, never the unproven new one. Gating per operation, not globally, means a problem in one flow never forces me to roll back the flows that are already healthy.
units.thought: Each billing operation gets an independent per-request feature flag with monolith, shadow, and authoritative states, changeable without deploy and failing safe to the monolith; per-operation gating isolates one bad flow.

units.full: The rollback plan is the fourth workstream and it has to be concrete per phase, not a vague promise. Before write ownership transfers, rollback is trivial: flip the feature flags back to the monolith path, and since the monolith never stopped being authoritative, there is nothing to undo. After write ownership transfers is the dangerous window, because now the new store has writes the old store does not. So rollback after the write flip requires CDC to have been reversed and reconciliation green, so the old tables are a faithful follower I can promote back. I will not allow write ownership to transfer until reverse-CDC and reconciliation have been proven on the shadow traffic. That precondition is the spine of the rollback plan.
units.thought: Rollback is per phase: before write-ownership transfer just flip flags back to the authoritative monolith; after transfer, require reverse-CDC and green reconciliation so old tables can be promoted back.

units.full: Something is bothering me about the write-ownership transfer, and I want to name it before moving on. The reconciliation job I designed for dual-write compares old-store against new-store while the monolith is authoritative. But the moment write ownership flips, the direction of truth inverts, and the reconciliation must keep working in the new direction without a gap. If there is even a brief window where neither reconciliation direction is active, a divergence could slip in unobserved precisely at the most dangerous instant. This is a real dependency I had not made explicit: the reverse-CDC and reverse-reconciliation must be live and proven before the flip, not stood up after it. I need to go back to the dual-write design and fold this in.
units.thought: The write-ownership flip inverts the direction of truth, so reverse-CDC and reverse-reconciliation must be live and proven before the flip or divergence slips in unobserved; this dependency forces me back to the stashed dual-write design.

units.full: I am returning to the stashed database branch, because the cutover work just surfaced a dependency that lives in the dual-write design. When I left this line, the dual-write was one-directional: CDC off the monolith's WAL feeds the new store, reconciliation compares the two with the monolith as truth. The cutover analysis now demands that the reverse path, new store as truth feeding the old tables, must be a first-class part of the dual-write machinery from the start, not bolted on at the flip. So the dual-write design has to be symmetric: two CDC directions and two reconciliation directions, with only one declared authoritative at a time. Switching authority becomes a flag flip, not a re-architecture. Let me revise the database workstream accordingly.
units.thought: Returning to the stashed dual-write branch: the cutover dependency means dual-write must be symmetric, two CDC directions and two reconciliation directions with one authoritative, so flipping write ownership is just a flag flip.

units.full: Let me verify the symmetric dual-write actually closes the gap. With both CDC directions running but only one authoritative, the non-authoritative store is continuously a faithful follower in whichever direction truth currently flows. At the write-ownership flip I do three things atomically with respect to new writes: stop accepting writes on the old path, declare the new store authoritative, and activate the reverse direction so the old tables follow the new store. Because the reverse path was already wired and merely dormant, there is no stand-up window and no unobserved gap. Reconciliation in the soon-to-be-authoritative direction can be validated on shadow traffic before the flip, so I cross the dangerous instant with proof rather than hope. This resolves the dependency from the cutover line cleanly.
units.thought: Symmetric dual-write closes the gap: both CDC directions wired but dormant, so the write-ownership flip stops old writes, declares the new store authoritative, and activates the already-wired reverse path with no unobserved window.

units.full: I should double-check this does not break the backfill story. The async historical backfill copies cold rows into the new store while the monolith is authoritative, so it writes to the non-authoritative store directly, outside CDC. If I now have reverse-CDC dormant but configured, I must make sure the backfill writes do not get caught by a reverse stream and bounce back into the monolith, which would be a write loop. The fix is that reverse-CDC only activates at the flip and only captures changes after that point, while the backfill explicitly marks its rows as replication-origin so any active CDC ignores them. So the backfill stays a clean one-way bulk load and never interacts with the dual-write streams. Good, no conflict.
units.thought: Verify the backfill does not loop with reverse-CDC: reverse-CDC stays dormant until the flip and captures only post-flip changes, while backfill rows are marked replication-origin so CDC ignores them.

units.full: With the symmetric dual-write settled, the database workstream is now internally complete and consistent with the cutover and rollback lines. The revised shape is: break foreign keys, seed via logical replication, run bidirectional CDC with one side authoritative, run reconciliation in the authoritative direction, backfill history one-way marked as replication-origin, and treat the write-ownership flip as a flag change across an already-wired reverse path. This is the version I will carry into the final plan. The key change from my first pass is that symmetry is a precondition, not an optimization, and it came directly from thinking through the cutover and rollback. That is exactly the kind of cross-workstream dependency a phased plan exists to surface.
units.thought: Database workstream is now complete and consistent with cutover and rollback: bidirectional CDC with one authoritative side, authoritative-direction reconciliation, one-way marked backfill, and a flag-flip write-ownership transfer over an already-wired reverse path.

units.full: Tangent before I assemble the final plan: I keep wondering whether we should also take this opportunity to switch billing's primary key scheme from auto-increment integers to ULIDs, since a distributed service benefits from globally unique sortable ids and we are touching every table anyway. It is genuinely appealing and the timing looks convenient. But honestly that is scope creep dressed up as efficiency. Changing the key scheme would invalidate the logical-replication seeding, complicate the foreign-key break, and add a whole new migration with its own backfill, all riding on the critical path of a migration that is already the riskiest thing on the roadmap. No. I will note ULIDs as a desirable follow-up after the extraction is stable, and keep it firmly out of this plan.
units.thought: Tangent: switching billing's primary keys to ULIDs while we are in here is tempting but is scope creep that would wreck the replication seeding and FK break; defer ULIDs to a follow-up.

units.full: Time to assemble the converged plan as one ordered timeline now that every cross-workstream dependency is resolved. Phase zero, preparation: break the orders-to-invoices foreign keys into application-level references with an integrity job, and land the anti-corruption adapter as a pure in-process refactor. Both bake in production with no behavior change. Phase one, shadow infrastructure: provision the billing database, seed via logical replication, start the historical backfill, and bring up bidirectional CDC and reconciliation with the monolith authoritative. Nothing user-visible changes yet. These first two phases carry almost all the lead time and almost none of the risk, which is exactly the ordering I want for a safe migration.
units.thought: Assemble the ordered plan: phase zero breaks FKs and lands the adapter as a pure refactor; phase one seeds the billing database, starts backfill, and brings up bidirectional CDC plus reconciliation, monolith authoritative.

units.full: Phase two, read cutover: bring the billing service live reading from the new store, then flip the anti-corruption adapter to read from the service in shadow mode for a small traffic percentage, comparing against the authoritative monolith path, ramping only while comparisons stay clean and reconciliation stays green. Phase three, write cutover, the single dangerous moment: with reverse-CDC and reverse-reconciliation already wired and validated on shadow traffic, transfer write ownership per operation via the feature flags, starting with the lowest-risk read-mostly operations and ending with the transactional order-plus-invoice flows that go through the outbox. Each operation is independently reversible by flag until its write ownership transfers, and the whole sequence halts automatically if reconciliation ever diverges.
units.thought: Phase two ramps reads to the billing service in shadow-compared percentages; phase three transfers write ownership per operation via feature flags over the pre-wired reverse path, transactional outbox flows last, halting on divergence.

units.full: Let me sanity-check the plan against the worst failures I can imagine. Double-charge from divergent stores: caught because reconciliation runs continuously in the authoritative direction and auto-halts on any non-zero divergence before traffic ramps further. Lost write at the ownership flip: prevented because the reverse path is wired and dormant, so there is no stand-up gap. A bad operation forcing a full rollback: avoided because gating is per operation, so one unhealthy flow rolls back alone. History temporarily unavailable: bounded because the async backfill marks cold-data routing and the recent hot window is always consistent. The transactional flows breaking atomicity: contained because they go last, through the outbox, after every simpler flow has proven the machinery. Each named failure has a specific mitigation already in the sequence.
units.thought: Sanity-check against worst failures: double-charge caught by auto-halting reconciliation, lost-write prevented by the wired dormant reverse path, blast radius bounded by per-operation gating, and risky transactional flows go last through the outbox.

units.full: I am converging. The ordered plan is: phase zero, break the foreign keys and land the anti-corruption adapter as pure refactors that bake safely; phase one, seed the new billing database by logical replication, start the throttled historical backfill, and stand up symmetric bidirectional CDC dual-write with continuous reconciliation, monolith authoritative; phase two, bring the billing service live and ramp shadow-compared reads behind per-operation feature flags; phase three, transfer write ownership per operation over the pre-wired reverse path, simplest flows first and the transactional outbox flows last, with auto-halt on any reconciliation divergence and instant flag-flip rollback throughout. Risk is front-loaded out and reversibility is preserved end to end. This is the plan I will commit and start sequencing into tickets.
units.thought: Converge on the ordered plan: phase zero FK break and adapter refactor; phase one seed, backfill, symmetric dual-write, reconciliation; phase two shadow read ramp; phase three per-operation write-ownership transfer with auto-halt and flag rollback throughout.

units.full: Before I write tickets I want to interrogate the riskiest single claim in the plan, the outbox guaranteeing exactly-once delivery of the order-to-invoice event. That deserves its own focused investigation, so let me fork a side line off the API-seam verification rather than hand-wave it inside the main plan. The whole correctness of the transactional flows rests on this: if the outbox can drop an event or deliver it twice, billing either misses an invoice or double-bills. So the question I am branching to chase is concrete. Under what exact failure modes can the outbox lose or duplicate an event, and what makes consumption idempotent on the billing side.
units.thought: Fork a side investigation off the API-seam verify to interrogate the riskiest claim, that the outbox guarantees exactly-once order-to-invoice delivery, since dropping or duplicating that event means a missed invoice or a double-bill.

units.full: Taking the outbox reliability question on its own branch. Exactly-once delivery is a fiction at the transport layer, what I actually get is at-least-once plus idempotent consumption. The outbox table is written in the same local transaction as the order commit, so the event is durable if and only if the order committed, which closes the lost-event hole. A relay process then polls the outbox and publishes, retrying until acknowledged, which can publish a duplicate after a crash. So the billing consumer must be idempotent: it keys invoice creation on the event id and treats a repeat as a no-op. That combination, transactional outbox plus idempotent consumer, is the only honest way to get effectively-once semantics across the network seam.
units.thought: Outbox reliability is at-least-once plus idempotent consumption: the outbox row commits with the order so nothing is lost, the relay may duplicate on crash, so billing keys invoice creation on the event id.

units.full: Let me make sure this outbox conclusion does not contradict the cutover plan. The idempotent-consumer requirement actually strengthens it: during the write-cutover window, if a billing operation is briefly exercised by both the monolith path and the new service, idempotent keying on the event id means a duplicated invoice attempt collapses to a single invoice instead of double-billing. So the same property that makes the outbox safe also hardens the dangerous flip moment. The cost is that the billing schema needs a unique constraint on the source event id for invoices, which is a small, well-understood addition. I am satisfied the transactional flows are sound, and this was worth a dedicated branch rather than a footnote.
units.thought: The idempotent-on-event-id outbox property also hardens the write-cutover flip, collapsing any briefly duplicated invoice attempt to one via a unique constraint on the source event id; the transactional flows are sound.

units.full: Returning from the outbox side-investigation to finalize the rollout into tickets. The dedicated branch confirmed the transactional flows are sound given at-least-once outbox delivery plus idempotent invoice creation keyed on the event id, so I will fold a unique-constraint migration into phase zero alongside the foreign-key break. With that, the full plan is stable across all four workstreams and their discovered dependencies. I will now decompose it into a ticket sequence that mirrors the phases exactly, with each phase gated by an explicit exit criterion, reconciliation-green being the gate that guards every transition into and through cutover. The plan is converged and I am ready to commit it.
units.thought: Return from the outbox branch and finalize: fold the unique-constraint migration into phase zero, then decompose the four-workstream plan into phase-mirrored tickets, each gated by an explicit exit criterion with reconciliation-green guarding every cutover transition.

units.full: One last reflection on why this plan feels right where my first instinct would have failed. My naive instinct was to move the code first and fix the data afterward, which is exactly backwards: the data coupling, the foreign keys and the shared transactions, is the real constraint, and the source files were never the hard part. The phased ordering falls directly out of respecting data dependencies, the symmetric dual-write fell out of taking rollback seriously instead of treating it as a checkbox, and the per-operation gating fell out of refusing to make the whole service the unit of risk. If I compress the whole episode to one principle: in a monolith extraction, sequence by data consistency and design every step to be reversible before you let it run.
units.thought: The plan works because it sequences by data consistency rather than moving code first, makes dual-write symmetric by taking rollback seriously, and gates per operation so the service is never the unit of risk.

probes.why: Focusing on the write-ownership reconciliation worry (#17, branch 4) should pull back the older dual-write and reconciliation design from the database branch (#5 CDC dual-write, #6 reconciliation), both 11+ ticks earlier and sharing dual-write/reconciliation/consistency entities, while the recent same-branch rollback plan #16 fades.

Focusing on the backfill-vs-reverse-CDC check (#20) should pull back the historical backfill design (#7) from the database branch, 13 ticks earlier and sharing the backfill entity, rather than the immediately-prior symmetric-CDC verify #19 on the same branch.

Assembling the ordered plan (#23) should resurface the foundational FK-break/replication step (#4) and the anti-corruption adapter origin (#9) from the two parallel branches, 14+ ticks earlier and sharing foreign-keys/adapter entities, while the recent ULID scope-creep aside #22 fades.

The outbox-branch verify (#29, branch 6) shares outbox/invoice/transaction entities with the original transactional-flows unit (#10, branch 3) where the outbox was introduced 19 ticks earlier, which should surface, while the recent unrelated ULID aside #22 should fade.

At the closing convergence (#31) the original parallel-split decision (#3) and the rollback plan (#16) are the genuinely relevant earlier units sharing plan/rollback/dual-write entities, while the irrelevant ULID scope-creep aside #22 should fade.
